Field of Greens vs AG1: Which Greens Powder Actually Earns Your Trust in 2026?

en.commentary: I have scooped enough of these into a shaker at 6am to have a firm opinion, and here it is: AG1 Next Gen is still the one to beat. Eighty-three ingredients, NSF Certified for Sport batch testing, and a formula that covers vitamins, minerals, probiotics and adaptogens in a single scoop earns the top spot on completeness alone. It costs $79 a month, and I understand why that stops a lot of people cold. That is exactly why the ranking underneath it matters. Live it Up Super Greens delivers a clean, readable label and real probiotics at $1.33 a serving on subscription, and for most people chasing a daily greens habit that they will actually keep, it is the smartest money in the category. I do want buyers to know Live it Up recalled specific lots in January 2026 over a salmonella concern, so check your lot and buy fresh. Field of Greens takes the evidence crown this year: its clinical trial was published in Frontiers in Nutrition in February 2026, and a peer-reviewed result is worth more than any influencer testimonial. Huel Daily Greens is the balanced daily driver, and Transparent Labs wins for anyone who reads labels and refuses proprietary blends. Buy for the reason that fits your morning, and skip the idea that the most expensive tub is automatically the best one.

highlights.title: AG1 Next Gen earns the top spot on formula and testing, and I stand by the price
highlights.body: AG1 packs 83 ingredients and carries NSF Certified for Sport batch testing, which means each production run is screened for banned substances and label accuracy. That combination of breadth and verification is unmatched, and it is why I rank it first. At $79 a month on subscription it is the priciest tub here, and I only recommend it to people who will drink it daily and value the single-scoop convenience of a full multivitamin, probiotic and greens dose in one.

highlights.title: Live it Up is the value pick I hand to friends, with one caveat
highlights.body: At $1.33 per serving on subscription, Live it Up Super Greens costs a fraction of the flagship while delivering 20-plus wholefood greens, 5 billion CFU of probiotics and a short label you can actually read. For building a habit that sticks, price per serving matters more than ingredient count, and this wins that math. The caveat is real: Live it Up recalled specific lots in January 2026 over a potential salmonella issue, so verify your lot number and buy a fresh pouch.

highlights.title: Field of Greens is the only one with peer-reviewed proof this year
highlights.body: In February 2026 BrickHouse published a clinical trial on Field of Greens in Frontiers in Nutrition, showing shifts in gut microbiome taxa and biological-aging markers. Most greens brands lean on ingredient stories, so an actual peer-reviewed result puts Field of Greens in its own tier for anyone who wants evidence over marketing. At roughly $3 a serving it is a premium buy, and the research is what justifies it.

highlights.title: Huel and Transparent Labs cover the two buyers AG1 leaves behind
highlights.body: Huel Daily Greens spreads 91 ingredients across greens, vitamins, probiotics and mushrooms for $1.50 a serving, making it the most balanced everyday formula that does not demand a flagship budget. Transparent Labs Prebiotic Greens goes the other way with a fully disclosed, no-proprietary-blend formula built around prebiotic fiber and enzymes. If you read supplement labels line by line, that transparency is the whole reason to buy.

en.commentary: AG1 Next Gen holds my top spot, and the fresh round of dietitian testing this week reinforces why. Reviewers gave the reformulated powder full marks on solubility, noting it now dissolves completely where the old version used to settle at the bottom of the glass, and the taste landed a 5 out of 5 as mild and earthy with a hint of vanilla. That reformulation is the story for me. It packs 75 ingredients into one scoop with the most comprehensive formula and cleanest third-party testing on this board, which is why it earns its 9.8 formula and 9.7 testing scores. The honest caveat, and the reason it does not run away with the ranking, is cost. At 79 dollars a month it is a real commitment, and dietitians are candid that the average person who eats a decent diet does not need it. That is exactly why Live It Up Super Greens stays at two. It delivers a genuinely enjoyable daily greens habit at a fraction of the price, and its 9.6 value score reflects that. Field of Greens holds third on the strength of its clinical evidence, the best on the board at 9.6, which matters if you want research behind the claims rather than a long ingredient list. Huel Daily Greens keeps fourth as the balanced pick with a strong formula and fair price. Nothing this week justified a reshuffle, so the order stands. My practical take is unchanged. If you train hard and want maximum coverage, AG1 earns its price. If you just want a pleasant daily greens routine, look one or two rows down and keep your money.

en.commentary: Greens powders held steady this week, and the latest testing reinforces why. Fortune ran fourteen products and again landed on AG1 Next Gen as best overall, which matches my number one. With 83 ingredients spanning superfoods, adaptogens, enzymes, prebiotics and probiotics, plus a taste and mixability that testers scored a perfect five, it remains the most complete daily greens I can point people to. The price stays its one real weakness at roughly 3.30 dollars a serving, and that is exactly why the value picks below it stay so appealing. Live It Up Super Greens keeps second as the taste-and-value alternative that most people can stick with, and Field of Greens holds a strong third on the back of published evidence. Its clinical research appeared in Frontiers in Nutrition in February 2026, showing gut microbiome shifts and measurable biological-aging improvements in over 60 percent of participants, which is the kind of peer-reviewed backing this category rarely gets. That evidence is why I keep its clinical score the highest on the board. Huel Daily Greens stays fourth as the formula-forward pick, and Transparent Labs Prebiotic Greens fifth for gut-health focus with clean testing. Further down, Amazing Grass and Nested Naturals remain the budget heroes, and Bloom the taste-first crowd-pleaser. No formula or testing news moved a rank this week, so I am holding the list. My advice stands, match the powder to your priority, completeness, evidence, taste, or price, and buy the one you will drink every morning.

references.title: AG1 study finds greens powder increases circulation of multiple micronutrients
AG1 Adds Creatine in Push Beyond Greens Powder
Greens powders are booming. But do they really work?

en.commentary:
AG1 published a bioavailability trial on July 23 and it is the first thing in a while that has moved my evidence score in this category. The design was straightforward: 16 healthy adults, 8 male and 8 female, took AG1 or placebo, with blood drawn at intervals afterward to measure circulating micronutrients. The result showed measurable increases in several of them.

I raised AG1's evidence mark from 8.5 to 8.6 and left everything else alone. That is a small step and it is small on purpose. Sixteen people is a pilot, the study is company funded, and showing that a vitamin you swallowed appears in your blood is a lower bar than showing it changed how you feel or function. What it does establish is that the powder's nutrients are actually absorbable rather than passing through, which is a fair question people ask about any blend this crowded. AG1 holds rank 1 on a 9.8 formula score and 9.7 on testing, and value stays at 7.0 because the subscription is still the most expensive habit on this list.

Field of Greens keeps the highest evidence mark in the category at 9.6, and I want to explain why that gap persists. Its formula is built on whole food concentrates with published research behind the specific extracts rather than a proprietary blend where doses are hidden. That is the harder path and it earns the number.

AG1 Pro, the creatine infused version launched in June, is not scored here as a separate product. My take is that it solves a stacking convenience problem rather than a nutrition one, and 5 grams of monohydrate from a plain tub costs a fraction of what the bundle charges.

If budget drives your decision, Live it Up at rank 2 with a 9.6 on value is where I would start. It delivers a solid greens base at a price you can sustain for a year, and consistency is what actually makes these powders worth anything.

highlights.title: AG1 evidence score rises to 8.6
highlights.body: The July 23 trial in 16 adults showed measurable increases in circulating micronutrients after AG1 versus placebo. It is a company funded pilot, so I moved the evidence factor by a single tenth and held the overall at 9.3 and rank 1.

highlights.title: Absorption is a fair question to ask of any blend
highlights.body: Greens powders pack dozens of ingredients into one scoop, and it is reasonable to wonder how much of that reaches your bloodstream. This is the first dataset I have seen from a major brand that measures it directly, which is why it counts even at small sample size.

highlights.title: Field of Greens still leads on evidence at 9.6
highlights.body: Its formula uses whole food concentrates with published research on the specific extracts and transparent dosing rather than a hidden proprietary blend. That transparency is why it holds the top evidence mark despite a 7.2 on value.

highlights.title: AG1 Pro bundles creatine for convenience
highlights.body: The June launch adds creatine, calcium HMB and zinc carnosine to the Next Gen base, aimed at the 30 percent of customers already taking creatine separately. Buying 5 grams of monohydrate in a plain tub costs far less if you are optimising for money rather than shelf space.

highlights.title: Live it Up is the sustainable daily choice
highlights.body: Rank 2 with a 9.6 on value and a 9.0 on taste. A greens powder only works if you drink it every morning for months, and this is the one whose price per serving makes that realistic for most people.

zh-tw.commentary:
AG1 在 7 月 23 日發表了一份生體可用率試驗，這是這個品類好一陣子以來第一件真的推動我調整證據分數的事。設計很直白：16 位健康成人，男女各 8 位，服用 AG1 或安慰劑，之後分時段抽血測血中微量營養素濃度，結果顯示其中幾項有可測得的上升。

我把 AG1 的證據分從 8.5 調到 8.6，其他都不動。這一步很小，而且是刻意的小。16 個人是先導試驗，研究由廠商出資，而且「吞下去的維生素出現在血液裡」這個門檻，本來就比「它改變了你的感受或機能」要低得多。它真正建立的是：這罐粉裡的營養素確實吸收得進去，不是原封不動排掉。對一個成分這麼擠的配方來說，這是大家會問的合理問題。AG1 維持第 1，配方 9.8、檢驗 9.7，性價比停在 7.0，因為那個訂閱制還是這份榜單上最貴的習慣。

Field of Greens 的證據分 9.6 仍然是全品類最高，我想解釋這個差距為什麼一直在。它的配方建立在全食物濃縮物上，而且是針對具體萃取物有發表研究，不是把劑量藏在專利配方裡。這條路難走，這個分數它拿得起。

6 月推出的 AG1 Pro 加了肌酸，這裡不另外列為產品評分。我的看法是它解決的是「懶得多開一罐」的方便性，不是營養問題，而 5 克單水肌酸單買一桶，成本只是這個組合包的零頭。

如果預算是你的決定因素，我會從第 2 名的 Live it Up 開始，性價比 9.6。它給你一個紮實的綠色基底，價格撐一年也不心痛，而能不能持續喝下去，才是這類粉末有沒有價值的關鍵。

highlights.title: AG1 證據分上調到 8.6
highlights.body: 7 月 23 日那份 16 人試驗顯示，服用 AG1 後血中多項微量營養素相對安慰劑有可測得的上升。屬於廠商出資的先導研究，所以我只把證據項目往上調 0.1，總分維持 9.3、名次維持第 1。

highlights.body: 綠粉一匙塞進幾十種原料，會好奇有多少真的進到血液裡很合理。這是我看過第一份大品牌直接量測這件事的資料，所以即使樣本數小，它還是有份量。

highlights.title: Field of Greens 證據分 9.6 依然領先
highlights.body: 它用的是全食物濃縮物，具體萃取物有發表研究，劑量也全部攤開，沒有藏在專利配方後面。這份透明度就是它在性價比只有 7.2 的情況下還能守住最高證據分的原因。

highlights.body: 6 月推出的版本在 Next Gen 基底上加了肌酸、鈣 HMB 跟鋅肌肽，瞄準那三成本來就另外吃肌酸的客戶。如果你在意的是錢而不是櫃子空間，單買一桶 5 克單水肌酸便宜太多了。

highlights.title: 能長期喝下去的選擇是 Live it Up
highlights.body: 第 2 名，性價比 9.6、口味 9.0。綠粉要有效果的前提是你每天早上真的喝，連喝好幾個月，而以每份價格來說，這罐是多數人做得到的那一個。
